exercises.title: Connect Mongoose to MongoDB and Wire Connection Events
exercises.problemHtml:
Mongoose keeps one shared connection pool per process, exposed as mongoose.connection. A production service must not only open it but also observe it: the initial connect can fail, and a healthy connection can drop later when the server restarts or the network blips. Those two failure modes surface through different channels — the first rejects the connect() promise, the second emits an error / disconnected event on the connection object.

Write a script that demonstrates the full lifecycle:

  • Print mongoose.connection.readyState before connecting (it is 0, disconnected).
  • Register listeners for the connected, error and disconnected events before calling connect, printing event: connected, event: error -> <err.name> and event: disconnected.
  • Connect with await mongoose.connect(uri, { serverSelectionTimeoutMS: 5000 }), then print the readyState (now 1) and the database name from mongoose.connection.name.
  • Show the other failure channel: open a separate connection to a dead port with mongoose.createConnection(...) and serverSelectionTimeoutMS: 300, await its .asPromise() inside a try/catch, print bad connect rejected: <err.name>, and close it so the process can exit.
  • Finish with await mongoose.disconnect() and print the final readyState.

The scaffold gives you the URI (read from MONGO_URL) and the main() wrapper; you add the listeners and the connect/disconnect calls.
exercises.inputSpec: No stdin. The connection string comes from process.env.MONGO_URL, defaulting to mongodb://127.0.0.1:27017/mg419_connect. A mongod must be reachable at that address.
exercises.outputSpec: Seven lines in order: "readyState before: 0", "event: connected", "readyState after: 1", "database: <db name>", "bad connect rejected: MongooseServerSelectionError", "event: disconnected", "readyState final: 0".
exercises.constraints: Use mongoose.connect for the default connection and mongoose.createConnection for the second one. Do not call connect inside a setTimeout or an event callback. Do not use the deprecated useNewUrlParser / useUnifiedTopology options — Mongoose 6+ ignores them.

examplesJson.explanation: The connected event fires while connect() is still awaiting, so it prints first; readyState 1 means the pool is usable.
An unreachable server makes server selection time out after 300 ms and the promise rejects — this failure never reaches the error event listener.
Closing the pool emits disconnected and returns readyState to 0.

hintsJson: There is exactly one default connection object; everything about its health hangs off it.
Attach the listeners before connect() runs, otherwise the connected event has already fired by the time you subscribe.
mongoose.connection.readyState is a number: 0 disconnected, 1 connected, 2 connecting, 3 disconnecting.
const bad = mongoose.createConnection(uri, { serverSelectionTimeoutMS: 300 }); try { await bad.asPromise(); } catch (err) { console.log("bad connect rejected:", err.name); } await bad.close();

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The whole exercise turns on one fact: mongoose.connection is a long-lived object that exists before any connection is opened, which is why you can read readyState (0) and subscribe to its events on line one. mongoose.connect() returns a promise that resolves once server selection succeeds and the pool is ready, so the connected listener necessarily fires before the line after the await — that ordering is what the expected output checks.

The key distinction is where each kind of failure appears. A failure to establish the connection in the first place rejects the promise: the driver spends up to serverSelectionTimeoutMS looking for a suitable server and then throws MongooseServerSelectionError. That rejection does not arrive as an error event, which is why a service that only registers connection.on('error') and forgets to await or .catch() the connect call silently starts up with a dead database. Conversely, a connection that drops after a successful start emits disconnected (and error), and the driver reconnects on its own — you should log these, not exit.

Two practical notes. createConnection gives you an independent pool, which is the right tool for a second database or for a probe like the one here; it returns the connection synchronously, and .asPromise() is how you await readiness. Always close such a connection, otherwise its retry timers keep the Node event loop alive and the process hangs. Finally, do not pass useNewUrlParser or useUnifiedTopology: they were removed as no-ops in Mongoose 6, and copying them from old tutorials is the most common source of noisy deprecation confusion.

exercises.title: Define a Schema and Model and Persist a Document
exercises.problemHtml:
MongoDB itself is schemaless; Mongoose adds the schema in your application layer. A Schema declares the shape, types and defaults of a document, a model is the class compiled from that schema, and an instance of the model is a document that knows how to save itself. Getting the three levels straight is the foundation of everything else in this module.

Build a Book model and write documents through it:

  • Declare bookSchema with title (String, required), author (String), pages (Number) and published (Boolean, default: false), plus the schema option { timestamps: true }.
  • Compile it with mongoose.model('Book', bookSchema) and print the collection Mongoose derived from the model name via Book.collection.name.
  • Create one document with new Book({...}) followed by await doc.save(), and a second with await Book.create({...}) — the two idioms that every codebase mixes.
  • Print, for the saved document: its title, the applied default for published, its __v version key, whether createdAt is a Date, and whether _id is a mongoose.Types.ObjectId.
  • Finish with Book.countDocuments() and a find() sorted by title, projecting only the titles, printed comma-separated.

Seed data is fixed by the scaffold: The Pragmatic Programmer (352 pages) and Refactoring (448 pages, published). The scaffold also drops the database first so re-runs are reproducible.
exercises.inputSpec: No stdin. The script connects to process.env.MONGO_URL, drops the database, then inserts the two books given in the scaffold.
exercises.outputSpec: Eight lines: "collection: books", "title: The Pragmatic Programmer", "published default: false", "__v: 0", "createdAt is Date: true", "id is ObjectId: true", "count: 2", "titles: Refactoring, The Pragmatic Programmer".
exercises.constraints: Use exactly one model named Book. Do not set _id yourself. Do not add createdAt/updatedAt fields by hand — use the timestamps schema option.

examplesJson.explanation: Mongoose lowercases and pluralises the model name to pick the collection, so Book maps to books.

examplesJson.input: const b = new Book({ title: 'The Pragmatic Programmer', author: 'Hunt and Thomas', pages: 352 }); await b.save();
examplesJson.explanation: published was not supplied so the schema default fills it in, and every freshly saved document starts at version 0.
Ascending sort on title puts Refactoring first; select limits the projection to the title field.

hintsJson: Schema describes, model compiles, document instance saves — three separate things.
Defaults and timestamps are declared on the schema, not applied by your code.
new Model(obj) then await doc.save() is equivalent to await Model.create(obj); create is a shortcut that does both.
const bookSchema = new mongoose.Schema({ title: { type: String, required: true }, published: { type: Boolean, default: false } }, { timestamps: true });

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The three levels are visible in the code. new mongoose.Schema({...}) is pure declaration — it stores paths, their casters and their options, and touches no database. mongoose.model('Book', bookSchema) compiles that schema into a class bound to the default connection; the model name is also how the collection is chosen, by lowercasing and pluralising it, which is why Book.collection.name prints books. An instance of that class is a document with its own change tracking, so book.save() knows whether to issue an insert or an update.

new Book(...) + save() and Book.create(...) are interchangeable; create simply does both steps and returns the saved document (or an array of them when given an array). Prefer the two-step form when you need to mutate or inspect the document between construction and persistence.

Three details are worth internalising. Defaults are applied at construction time, not at save time, so book.published is already false before the write hits the server. timestamps: true makes Mongoose maintain createdAt and updatedAt for you on saves and on updates — hand-rolled date fields drift the moment someone updates through a query. And __v is the version key Mongoose uses for optimistic concurrency on array updates; it starts at 0 and is not something your application code should set. The classic beginner error is calling mongoose.model('Book', ...) twice for the same name, which throws OverwriteModelError; models are singletons per connection, so define each one exactly once in its own module and require it everywhere else.

exercises.title: Schema Validation with enum, min, and a Custom Validator
exercises.problemHtml:
Validation is the main reason teams adopt an ODM over the raw driver. Mongoose runs validators automatically before every save(), and when one fails it throws a single ValidationError whose errors object is keyed by path — exactly the shape an HTTP layer needs to build a field-by-field 422 response.

Model a product catalogue entry and then deliberately break it:

  • Declare productSchema with: sku (String, required, plus a custom validator accepting only the pattern three uppercase letters, a hyphen and four digits, with the message SKU must look like ABC-1234); price (Number, required, min: 0); category (String, enum of book, tool, toy); stock (Number, default: 0); and discount (Number, default 0) with a custom validator that rejects a discount greater than the document's own price, message discount cannot exceed price.
  • Save a valid product { sku: 'ABC-1234', price: 20, category: 'tool' } and print its defaulted stock.
  • Try to save { sku: 'abc1', price: -5, category: 'gadget' } inside try/catch. Print name: the error's name, then paths: the sorted keys of err.errors comma-separated, then the message of the sku error and the kind of the price error. Four paths fail, not three — work out why before you run it.
  • Try to save { sku: 'XYZ-0001', price: 10, category: 'toy', discount: 15 } and print the cross-field validator's message.
  • Finally print saved count: the number of documents actually in the collection.

The custom validator on discount must be a regular function, not an arrow function, because it needs this to reach the sibling price path. The scaffold connects, drops the database and provides the three save attempts.
exercises.inputSpec: No stdin. Three documents are attempted in order: a valid one, one violating sku/price/category, and one violating the cross-field discount rule.
exercises.outputSpec: Lines: "stock default: 0"; "name: ValidationError"; "paths: category, discount, price, sku"; "sku message: SKU must look like ABC-1234"; "price kind: min"; "discount message: discount cannot exceed price"; "saved count: 1".
exercises.constraints: Validation must come from the schema — no manual if-checks before save. The discount validator must be a normal function so this refers to the document. Do not catch and swallow errors silently.

examplesJson.input: await new Product({ sku: 'abc1', price: -5, category: 'gadget' }).save()
examplesJson.output:
name: ValidationError
paths: category, discount, price, sku
examplesJson.explanation: One ValidationError carries every failing path at once. discount fails too: its default of 0 is greater than the invalid price of -5, so the cross-field validator rejects it as well.
Each entry is a ValidatorError whose kind names the rule that failed — useful for machine-readable API errors.

examplesJson.input: await new Product({ sku: 'XYZ-0001', price: 10, category: 'toy', discount: 15 }).save()
examplesJson.output: discount message: discount cannot exceed price
examplesJson.explanation: The custom validator reads this.price, so it can compare two paths of the same document.

hintsJson: Every failing path ends up in one error object, not one error per field.
A field definition accepts validate: { validator, message } alongside type and required.
err.errors is an object keyed by path; Object.keys(err.errors).sort() gives you a stable list.
sku: { type: String, required: true, validate: { validator: (v) => /^[A-Z]{3}-\d{4}$/.test(v), message: 'SKU must look like ABC-1234' } }

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Mongoose validates the whole document before it writes anything, collecting all failures rather than aborting on the first one. That is why the second attempt yields a single ValidationError whose errors map contains three entries — sku (custom validator), price (min) and category (enum). Each entry is a ValidatorError exposing message, path, value and kind; kind is the machine-readable rule name (required, min, enum, user defined), which is what you should branch on in an error-mapping middleware rather than string-matching messages.

The cross-field rule is the part that trips people up. A validator declared as an arrow function inherits this from the enclosing module scope, so this.price is undefined and the comparison silently misbehaves. Written as a normal function, this is the document being validated on a save(), and the rule works. The same caveat has a second edge: on an update path, this is not a document, so validators that depend on siblings only fire correctly when you save a document — a strong argument for routing writes through save() rather than findOneAndUpdate when business rules span fields.

The four-path result is worth pausing on. You supplied three bad values, but discount also fails: its default of 0 is applied at construction time and then validated like any other path, and 0 <= -5 is false. Defaults are not exempt from validation, and a cross-field validator can therefore fail because of a different field's bad value — a cascade you will see often in real forms.

Note also what did not happen: nothing was written for the two failed attempts, so countDocuments() is 1. Validation runs client-side, inside your Node process, before any network call. That is fast and gives rich errors, but it also means the database is not enforcing these rules — a write from mongosh or another service bypasses them entirely. Constraints you truly cannot violate (uniqueness above all) belong in an index, which the later exercises cover.

exercises.title: Nested Schemas and Arrays of Subdocuments
exercises.problemHtml:
MongoDB's superpower is storing a whole aggregate in one document, and Mongoose models that with subdocuments: a schema embedded in another schema, either as a single nested object or as an array. Subdocuments are full documents — they get their own _id, run their own validators, and their errors are reported under a dotted path such as items.1.qty.

Model an order that carries its shipping address and its line items inline:

  • Declare addressSchema with street, city and zip, all required, and the option { _id: false } — an address is a value, not an entity.
  • Declare itemSchema with name (required), qty (Number, required, min: 1) and price (Number, required).
  • Declare orderSchema with customer (String, required), shipping: addressSchema and items: [itemSchema].
  • Create an order for ada shipping to 12 Ly Thai To, Hanoi, 10000 with one item { name: 'Keyboard', qty: 1, price: 30 }, then push a second item { name: 'Cable', qty: 3, price: 5 } onto order.items and save again.
  • Print: the number of items; whether the first item has its own _id; whether shipping has an _id; the result of looking the second item up by its id with order.items.id(...) — print its qty; and the order total computed from the line items.
  • Then push an invalid item { name: 'Mat', qty: 0, price: 9 }, attempt to save inside try/catch, and print the single failing path from err.errors.

The scaffold connects, drops the database and supplies the literal order data.
exercises.inputSpec: No stdin. One order document is built in memory from the literals in the scaffold, saved, extended, then given a deliberately invalid third line item.
exercises.outputSpec: Lines: "items: 2"; "item has _id: true"; "shipping has _id: false"; "second item qty: 3"; "total: 45"; "shipping city: Hanoi"; "invalid path: items.2.qty".
exercises.constraints: Embed the line items — do not create a separate Item collection. Use the DocumentArray helper id() to find a subdocument, not a manual find over the array. Compute the total in JavaScript with reduce.

examplesJson.input: order.items.push({ name: 'Cable', qty: 3, price: 5 }); await order.save();
examplesJson.explanation: Pushing a plain object casts it to a subdocument, which is given its own ObjectId automatically.
A subdocument array exposes id() for O(n) lookup by subdocument _id — the standard way to address one line of an order.

examplesJson.input: order.items.push({ name: 'Mat', qty: 0, price: 9 }); await order.save();
examplesJson.explanation: The min: 1 validator on the subdocument fails and is reported under the dotted path of its index in the array.

hintsJson: A schema can be used as the type of a field, or as the element type of an array field.
Options belong on the embedded schema: { _id: false } suppresses the generated id for a value object.
order.items is a DocumentArray, so it has extra methods beyond a plain array — id() among them.
const orderSchema = new mongoose.Schema({ customer: { type: String, required: true }, shipping: addressSchema, items: [itemSchema] });

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Using a schema as a field type is what turns a plain nested object into a real subdocument. items: [itemSchema] produces a DocumentArray: pushing a plain object casts and validates it, gives it an _id, and lets you address it later with order.items.id(subId). That id-based addressing is what makes "update line 2 of this order" expressible, both in memory and in a positional query such as { 'items._id': subId } with $set: { 'items.$.qty': 5 }.

The { _id: false } on addressSchema encodes a design decision, not a micro-optimisation: a shipping address is a value object that only exists as part of its order, so it needs no identity. Line items, by contrast, keep their ids because the application refers to them individually.

The validation behaviour is the real lesson. Subdocument validators run as part of the parent's save(), and failures are reported under the dotted path including the array index — items.2.qty. An HTTP layer can therefore return field-level errors for deeply nested form data without any custom mapping. Two traps follow from this. First, because the whole document is validated on every save, an invalid item added late blocks the entire order from saving, including changes you made to other fields — the document in memory is now dirty and out of sync with the database. Second, embedding is bounded by the 16 MB document limit and by write contention on the parent: embed when the children are read and written with the parent and their number stays small, and use references (the next exercise) when they grow without bound or are queried independently.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  Order[Order document] --> Customer[customer string]
  Order --> Shipping[shipping nested addressSchema no id]
  Order --> Items[items DocumentArray]
  Items --> I0[item 0 with own ObjectId]
  Items --> I1[item 1 with own ObjectId]
  I1 --> Validate[qty min 1 validator runs on parent save]

exercises.title: References and populate: select, Nested Paths, and the Query Count
exercises.problemHtml:
When data should not be embedded you store an ObjectId and declare what it points at with ref. populate() then swaps those ids for the referenced documents. It looks like a SQL join, but it is not one: Mongoose issues extra queries — one per populated path — and stitches the results together in Node. Knowing that is the difference between a fast endpoint and an accidental fan-out.

Build a three-level catalogue and measure it:

  • Models: Publisher (name), Author (name, publisher as ObjectId with ref: 'Publisher'), Book (title, author as ObjectId with ref: 'Author').
  • Seed one publisher Chilton, two authors Frank Herbert and Ursula Le Guin both pointing at it, and three books: Dune and Children of Dune by Herbert, A Wizard of Earthsea by Le Guin.
  • Read one book without populate and print whether book.author is an ObjectId.
  • Install a query counter with mongoose.set('debug', () => { queries++; }), reset the counter, then fetch all three books sorted by title with a nested populate: path author, select 'name -_id', and an inner populate of publisher with select 'name -_id'.
  • Print the counter, then one line per book: <title> / <author name> / <publisher name>.
  • Print whether the populated author object still exposes _id (it must not, because of the -_id in the select).

The scaffold connects, drops the database, seeds the documents and declares the counter variable.
exercises.inputSpec: No stdin. Seed data: 1 publisher (Chilton), 2 authors referencing it, 3 books referencing the authors — created by the scaffold.
exercises.outputSpec: Lines: "unpopulated author is ObjectId: true"; "queries: 3"; then "A Wizard of Earthsea / Ursula Le Guin / Chilton", "Children of Dune / Frank Herbert / Chilton", "Dune / Frank Herbert / Chilton"; then "author has _id: false".
exercises.constraints: Use a single find() plus populate — no manual loop issuing one query per book. The select strings must suppress _id. Do not use $lookup here.

examplesJson.explanation: Without populate the ref field holds only the raw ObjectId that was stored.

examplesJson.input: Book.find().sort({ title: 1 }).populate({ path: 'author', select: 'name -_id', populate: { path: 'publisher', select: 'name -_id' } })
examplesJson.explanation: One query for the books, one for the authors (batched with $in), one for the publishers — three round trips, not one join and not one query per book.
After populate the ref fields are hydrated documents, so nested property access works.

hintsJson: A ref field stores only an id; something has to go and fetch the other document.
populate accepts an options object, and that object can itself contain a populate for the next level.
select: 'name -_id' keeps name and drops the always-included _id.
mongoose.set('debug', () => { queries++; }) fires once per collection operation, so reset the counter immediately before the read you want to measure.

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
ref is metadata only: the stored value is a bare ObjectId, which is why the unpopulated read prints true for the instanceof check. populate() is a client-side join executed by Mongoose after the primary query returns. It collects the ids it saw, issues one query per populated path using $in, and assigns the results back onto the documents. Hence three queries here — books, authors, publishers — regardless of the fact that three books point at two authors that share one publisher. The counter makes this measurable rather than a claim.

The practical consequence is that populate cost scales with the number of populated paths and levels, not with the number of parent documents — but it is still additional round trips, and it happens outside the database, so the server cannot use them to filter or sort. You cannot sort books by author.name with populate, and filtering on a populated field only discards results after they have been fetched (a populated path that fails a match becomes null, leaving the parent in the array). When you need a true server-side join with filtering and sorting on the joined side, use an aggregation $lookup; when you need one document with its label fields, populate is simpler and often faster.

select: 'name -_id' narrows the payload of the populated documents — always do this on hot paths, because populating a full user document to render a display name is a classic source of oversized responses. Note the asymmetry: -_id is required because _id is included by default. Finally, remember mongoose.set('debug', ...) is a global switch that prints or, as here, counts every collection operation — invaluable for spotting an N+1 caused by populating inside a loop instead of populating one batched query.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
sequenceDiagram
  participant App as Node app
  participant DB as MongoDB
  App->>DB: find books sorted by title
  DB-->>App: 3 books with author ids
  App->>DB: find authors where _id in list
  DB-->>App: 2 authors with publisher ids
  App->>DB: find publishers where _id in list
  DB-->>App: 1 publisher
  App->>App: stitch documents together in memory

exercises.title: Virtuals: Computed Fields, Setters, and Virtual Populate
exercises.problemHtml:
A virtual is a schema path that is computed in Node and never stored. Two uses dominate real projects: presenting derived data such as a full name or a signed URL, and declaring a reverse relationship — "a user's posts" — without duplicating ids on the parent. Virtuals cost nothing on disk, but they are invisible to the database, and that has consequences you must know.

Build a User model that exercises both kinds:

  • userSchema has firstName and lastName (Strings). Add a virtual fullName with a getter returning the two joined by a space, and a setter that splits an assigned string on the first space into the two real paths. Use regular functions, not arrows.
  • Declare a virtual posts using { ref: 'Post', localField: '_id', foreignField: 'author' }, where postSchema is { title: String, author: ObjectId ref 'User' }.
  • Create user Ada Lovelace and print fullName. Then assign user.fullName = 'Grace Hopper' and print the resulting firstName and lastName.
  • Print whether JSON.stringify(user) contains fullName with the default schema options, then enable toJSON: { virtuals: true } on a second model built from the same fields and print the same check again.
  • Show the database cannot see virtuals: run User.countDocuments({ fullName: 'Grace Hopper' }) and print the result.
  • Create two posts by that user and one by another, then load the user with .populate('posts') and print how many posts came back and their titles sorted and comma-separated.

The scaffold connects, drops the database and declares both schemas without the virtuals.
exercises.inputSpec: No stdin. Two users and three posts are created by the script; posts reference their author by ObjectId.
exercises.outputSpec: Lines: "fullName: Ada Lovelace"; "after set: Grace / Hopper"; "json has fullName: false"; "json with virtuals has fullName: true"; "query by virtual matched: 0"; "posts: 2"; "titles: Notes on the Engine, Sketch of the Analytical Engine".
exercises.constraints: Do not store fullName as a real path. Do not store an array of post ids on the user — the reverse relation must come from the virtual. Getters and setters must be normal functions so this is the document.

examplesJson.explanation: The virtual setter writes through to the two real paths, so the document saves normally.
Virtuals exist only in Node — MongoDB has no such field, so the filter matches nothing.
Virtual populate runs a query on Post where author equals this user _id, without any array of ids stored on the user.

hintsJson: A schema path you never want written to disk is not a field at all.
schema.virtual(name).get(fn) and .set(fn) define the computed path; the setter assigns to this.firstName / this.lastName.
Virtuals are omitted from toJSON and toObject unless you pass { virtuals: true } in those schema options.
schema.virtual('posts', { ref: 'Post', localField: '_id', foreignField: 'author' }) — then populate('posts') like any ref.

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
A virtual is a property descriptor installed on the document class, so user.fullName runs your getter every time it is read and user.fullName = '...' runs your setter, which writes through to the real paths. Because the setter mutates firstName and lastName, the document saves exactly as if you had assigned them directly — a clean way to accept a legacy input shape without polluting storage. As with custom validators, the functions must be normal functions: an arrow function captures the module's this and the getter returns undefined undefined.

The two consequences of "never stored" are both demonstrated. First, virtuals are dropped by toJSON and toObject unless the schema opts in with { toJSON: { virtuals: true } } — the single most common surprise, because res.json(doc) in Express calls toJSON and your carefully computed field vanishes from the API response. Second, MongoDB has no idea the field exists, so countDocuments({ fullName: ... }) matches nothing: you cannot filter, sort, or index on a virtual. If you must query it, store a real (possibly denormalised) field and maintain it in a hook.

Virtual populate inverts a relationship without denormalising ids. Storing author on each post is the correct model — the many side owns the reference — but the parent still wants to read its children, and { ref, localField, foreignField } expresses precisely that: find Post documents whose author equals this user's _id. It is unbounded by document size, unlike an embedded array of ids, and it supports match, options such as sort and limit, and count: true when you only need a number. Like all populate, it costs an extra query and is not visible to the database, so it is unavailable inside aggregations — use $lookup there.

exercises.title: Instance Methods, Statics, and Reusable Query Helpers
exercises.problemHtml:
Mongoose gives you three distinct extension points, and choosing the right one keeps business logic out of route handlers. An instance method acts on one document (this is the document). A static acts on the collection (this is the model). A query helper extends the chainable query builder (this is the query), so it composes with sort, limit and further helpers.

Model a task list and add all three:

  • taskSchema: title (String, required), status (String, enum open/done, default open), dueAt (Date), owner (String).
  • Instance method markDone() that sets status to done and returns this.save().
  • Static findOverdue(now) returning every task whose status is open and whose dueAt is before now, sorted by dueAt ascending.
  • Query helper byOwner(owner) that adds { owner } to the current query with this.where.
  • Seed the five tasks given in the scaffold, then: call markDone() on renew domain and print its status after saving; print the titles returned by Task.findOverdue(new Date('2026-03-01')); print the titles of Task.find().byOwner('ada').sort({ title: 1 }); and print the count of Task.find({ status: 'open' }).byOwner('ada').

All three must be declared before mongoose.model() is called — the model is compiled from the schema at that moment. Every function must be a normal function, never an arrow.
exercises.inputSpec: No stdin. Five seed tasks created by the scaffold: pay rent (ada, due 2026-01-10), renew domain (ada, due 2026-02-01), write report (bob, due 2026-02-20), buy milk (ada, due 2026-06-01), archive logs (bob, already done, due 2026-01-05).
exercises.outputSpec: Lines: "after markDone: done"; "overdue: pay rent, write report"; "ada tasks: buy milk, pay rent, renew domain"; "ada open: 2".
exercises.constraints: No business logic in main() — the filters must live in the static and the query helper. Query helpers go on schema.query, statics on schema.statics, instance methods on schema.methods. Do not use arrow functions for any of them.

examplesJson.input: const task = await Task.findOne({ title: 'renew domain' }); await task.markDone();
examplesJson.explanation: An instance method mutates and saves the single document it was called on; this is that document.
renew domain was just marked done and buy milk is due in June, so only two open tasks are past the cutoff; the static sorts them by dueAt.
The helper appends a condition to a query already in progress, so filters compose instead of being duplicated.

hintsJson: Ask what each piece of logic operates on: one document, the whole collection, or a query being built.
schema.methods.x, schema.statics.x and schema.query.x are three different objects on the schema.
A query helper must return this so the chain continues; this.where({ owner }) does both.
taskSchema.statics.findOverdue = function (now) { return this.find({ status: 'open', dueAt: { $lt: now } }).sort({ dueAt: 1 }); };

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The three extension points differ only in what this is bound to, and that determines what each is good for. In markDone, this is the loaded document, so the method can mutate paths and call save() — returning the save promise lets the caller await it. In findOverdue, this is the model, so this.find(...) starts a fresh query; returning the query (not awaiting it) keeps the static composable, so a caller can still tack on .limit(10) or .lean(). In byOwner, this is the query being built, and this.where({ owner }) merges another condition into it and returns the query, which is what makes Task.find({ status: 'open' }).byOwner('ada') read like a sentence.

Trace the expected results. renew domain is marked done first, so by the time findOverdue(2026-03-01) runs only pay rent (10 Jan) and write report (20 Feb) are still open and past the cutoff; archive logs is excluded because it is already done, and buy milk because June is after the cutoff. The sort on dueAt puts January before February. Ada's tasks sorted by title give buy milk, pay rent, renew domain, and of those only two remain open after the mark-done.

Two traps deserve highlighting. Arrow functions break all three, because they capture the enclosing this instead of receiving Mongoose's binding — the symptom is a cryptic "cannot read properties of undefined" rather than a clear error. And everything must be attached before mongoose.model() is called: the model is compiled from the schema at that instant, so a static added afterwards will not exist on the model. Both mistakes are avoided by the standard file layout in which one module owns a schema, decorates it, compiles it and exports the model.

exercises.title: Middleware: pre-save Hashing versus Query Middleware
concepts: this in document vs query hooks
exercises.problemHtml:
Hooks are how cross-cutting rules — hashing a password, stamping an audit field, cascading a delete — stay in one place. The trap that catches nearly everyone is that Mongoose has two families of middleware. In document middleware (save, validate) this is the document. In query middleware (findOneAndUpdate, updateOne, find) this is the query: there is no document, save hooks do not run, and validators are off unless you ask for them.

Prove both behaviours on a User model. Use crypto.createHash('sha256') truncated to 16 hex characters as a stand-in for a real password hash so the output is deterministic.

  • userSchema: username (String, required), password (String, required, minlength: 8), role (String, enum user/admin, default user), lastHashedBy (String).
  • pre('save'): if this.isModified('password'), replace it with the hash and set lastHashedBy to 'save-hook'. Guarding on isModified is mandatory.
  • post('save'): print post save: <username>.
  • pre('findOneAndUpdate'): read the update with this.getUpdate(); if it sets a password, hash that value in place and also set lastHashedBy to 'query-hook' via this.setUpdate or by mutating the update object.
  • Create ada with password secret12; print the stored hash and confirm it equals hash('secret12'). Save again after changing only username and print whether the stored password changed (it must not).
  • Update through the query API with User.findOneAndUpdate({ username: 'ada2' }, { $set: { password: 'newsecret' } }, { new: true }); print the new hash, and print lastHashedBy to show which hook ran.
  • Finally show validators are off by default on queries. Update role to the invalid value root with no options, print role now: the stored value to prove the garbage landed in the database, then repeat the same update with { runValidators: true } in a try/catch and print the caught error name.

The scaffold provides the hash helper and the sequence of calls.
exercises.inputSpec: No stdin. One user (ada / secret12) is created, renamed, then updated twice through findOneAndUpdate. The hash helper is sha256 truncated to 16 hex characters.
exercises.outputSpec: Lines: "post save: ada"; "stored hash matches: true"; "post save: ada2"; "password unchanged on rename: true"; "after query update matches: true"; "lastHashedBy: query-hook"; "role now: root"; "with runValidators: ValidationError".
exercises.constraints: Hash only when the password path is modified — an unguarded hook double-hashes on every save and locks users out. Do not call save() inside a query hook. Do not use arrow functions for hooks.

examplesJson.explanation: The isModified guard makes a second save that only touched username leave the already-hashed password alone.
In query middleware this is the Query, so you rewrite the update object rather than a document — the save hook never fires for this path.
Query updates skip validators unless runValidators is passed; with it, the enum on role rejects the value that the previous call had happily written.

hintsJson: Two hooks look similar but receive completely different things in this.
In a save hook you mutate this.password; in a findOneAndUpdate hook there is no document to mutate.
this.getUpdate() returns the update object, e.g. { $set: { password: 'newsecret' } } — hash update.$set.password in place.
userSchema.pre('save', function (next) { if (!this.isModified('password')) return next(); this.password = hash(this.password); this.lastHashedBy = 'save-hook'; next(); });

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The pre('save') hook is document middleware: this is the document about to be written, so hashing means assigning to this.password. The isModified('password') guard is not an optimisation, it is a correctness requirement — without it the second save() (which only changed username) would hash the already hashed value, and every affected user would be permanently unable to log in. Mongoose tracks modified paths per document, which is exactly the information the guard needs. The post('save') hook receives the saved document and runs after the write succeeds, making it the right place for cache invalidation or event publishing; it fires twice here, once per save.

The findOneAndUpdate hook is query middleware and behaves differently in every respect. There is no document — this is the Query, and the payload lives in this.getUpdate(), typically shaped as { $set: { ... } }, which is why the hook reads update.$set (falling back to the bare object for a replacement-style update) and rewrites the value in place. Crucially, save hooks do not run for query updates: had you only written the save hook, a password changed through findOneAndUpdate would land in the database in plaintext. That single asymmetry is behind a large share of real-world Mongoose security bugs.

The last two calls demonstrate the second asymmetry: validators are document-level and are skipped by query updates unless you pass runValidators: true, which is why role: 'root' — a value the enum forbids — is written to the database by the first call and throws ValidationError on the second. An unvalidated update can therefore leave a document that its own schema considers invalid. Even then only the paths present in the update are validated, and this inside a validator is the query rather than a document, so cross-field rules cannot see siblings. The defensive strategy is consistent: for models with real invariants, funnel writes through save(), and if you must use query updates, register the equivalent query hooks and always pass runValidators: true.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[doc.save called] --> B[pre save hook, this is the document]
  B --> C[validators run automatically]
  C --> D[write to MongoDB]
  D --> E[post save hook receives the document]
  F[Model.findOneAndUpdate called] --> G[pre findOneAndUpdate hook, this is the query]
  G --> H[validators skipped unless runValidators true]
  H --> I[write to MongoDB]
  I --> J[save hooks never fire on this path]

exercises.title: Unique Indexes, Duplicate Key 11000, autoIndex, and lean()
exercises.problemHtml:
Two production realities meet in this exercise. First, unique: true in a Mongoose schema is not a validator — it is a request to build a unique index, and the enforcement happens in the database, which reports failure as a driver error with code 11000 rather than a ValidationError. Second, hydrating documents is not free: lean() skips it and returns plain objects, which is the standard optimisation for read-only endpoints.

Build an Account model and exercise both:

  • accountSchema: email (String, required), tenant (String, required), status (String, default active), displayName (String). Declare a unique index on email and a compound index on { tenant: 1, status: 1 } with schema.index(...), and set the schema option autoIndex: true explicitly.
  • Wait for index builds with await Account.init() before writing anything, then print the index names from Account.collection.indexes(), sorted and comma-separated.
  • Insert ada@example.com for tenant t1. Then attempt the same email again inside try/catch and print: the error name, its code, the offending field from err.keyValue, and a mapped user-facing message of the form email already registered: ada@example.com.
  • Show that the duplicate is not a ValidationError by printing is ValidationError: false.
  • Insert two more accounts, then compare read shapes: run one query normally and one with .lean(), printing whether each result is an instance of the model, whether it exposes save, and its constructor name.
  • Finally print the result of Account.syncIndexes() as its length — the list of dropped indexes, which is empty here.

The scaffold connects, drops the database and provides the seed literals.
exercises.inputSpec: No stdin. Three accounts on tenant t1: ada@example.com, grace@example.com, alan@example.com. A duplicate insert of ada@example.com is attempted deliberately.
exercises.outputSpec: Lines: "indexes: _id_, email_1, tenant_1_status_1"; "error name: MongoServerError"; "code: 11000"; "duplicate field: email"; "mapped: email already registered: ada@example.com"; "is ValidationError: false"; "hydrated is model: true"; "hydrated has save: true"; "lean is model: false"; "lean has save: false"; "lean constructor: Object"; "dropped indexes: 0".
exercises.constraints: Do not pre-check for an existing email with a findOne before inserting — that is a race condition; let the index reject the write and handle 11000. Await Model.init() before the first write so the index exists. Do not use unique as if it validated.

examplesJson.input: accountSchema.index({ email: 1 }, { unique: true }); await Account.init();
examplesJson.explanation: init() waits for autoIndex to finish building; every collection also has the implicit _id_ index.

examplesJson.input: await Account.create({ email: 'ada@example.com', tenant: 't1' }) // second time
examplesJson.explanation: The server rejects the write; err.keyValue names the field and value that collided, which is what you map to a 409 response.

examplesJson.output:
lean has save: false
lean constructor: Object
examplesJson.explanation: lean() returns the raw BSON-decoded object with no getters, virtuals, or document methods.

hintsJson: A rule the database enforces cannot be reported by the schema validator that runs in Node.
Duplicate key failures arrive as a MongoServerError with a numeric code, not as err.errors.
err.keyValue is an object like { email: "ada@example.com" }; Object.keys and Object.values give you the field and value.
try { await Account.create(doc); } catch (err) { if (err.code === 11000) { const [field, value] = Object.entries(err.keyValue)[0]; ... } }

starterCodeJson.name: solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The first half is about where a rule is enforced. required, min and custom validators run in your Node process and throw mongoose.Error.ValidationError. Uniqueness cannot work that way — two processes could both check and both insert — so it is delegated to a unique index, and a violation comes back from the server as a MongoServerError with code: 11000, carrying keyPattern and keyValue. That is why err instanceof mongoose.Error.ValidationError is false, and why every serious Express error handler needs a dedicated 11000 branch that maps Object.entries(err.keyValue)[0] to a 409 with a field-specific message. Pre-checking with findOne and then inserting is not a substitute: the gap between the two calls is a race, and under concurrency you get the 11000 anyway — with the pre-check merely hiding it most of the time.

Account.init() matters because index creation is asynchronous. With autoIndex on (the default), Mongoose fires off createIndex for every declared index when the model is first used, and init() returns the promise that settles when those builds finish. Insert before that resolves and the duplicate may slip through. In production the usual guidance is autoIndex: false: on a large collection an unexpected index build at boot can stall the server, and every replica running the same code races to build the same indexes. Instead you ship index changes deliberately — a migration step calling syncIndexes(), which creates what the schema declares and drops indexes the schema no longer has (returning their names; empty here, hence 0).

The second half is about hydration cost. A normal query decodes BSON and then builds a full Mongoose document per result: getters, virtuals, change tracking, validation machinery, save(). lean() skips all of it and hands you the plain object, which is measurably faster and much lighter in memory on large result sets — the reason it is the default choice for list endpoints that serialise straight to JSON. The trade-off is total: a lean result has no save(), no virtuals, no custom getters, no toJSON transforms, and its _id is a raw ObjectId. Reach for it when you are reading to render, and stay hydrated when you intend to modify.

exercises.title: Capstone: Transactional Order Service with Models in Separate Modules
exercises.problemHtml:
An order service is where every part of this module comes together: models live in their own module so routes stay thin, a placement must decrement stock and create the order atomically, a repeated order number must be rejected by the database, and the read-back must be cheap. Multi-document transactions require a replica set — a single-node replica set is the normal local setup and is what the connection string assumes.

Write two files.

models.js exports Product and Order:

  • Product: sku (String, required, unique index), name, price (Number, required, min: 0), stock (Number, required, min: 0).
  • Order: orderNumber (String, required, unique index), customer (String, required), lines — an array of subdocuments { product: ObjectId ref Product, qty: Number min 1, unitPrice: Number }, total (Number) and status (enum placed/cancelled, default placed), with timestamps. A pre('validate') hook computes total from the lines so no caller can get it wrong.

solution.js implements placeOrder({ orderNumber, customer, items }) and a demo main():

  • Open a session, run the work inside session.withTransaction, and always endSession() in a finally.
  • For each requested item: look the product up by sku with .session(session); if missing, throw unknown sku <sku>. Decrement with a conditional updateOne filtered on { _id, stock: { $gte: qty } } using $inc, and if modifiedCount is not 1 throw insufficient stock for <sku>.
  • Create the order with Order.create([doc], { session }) — the array form is required when passing a session.
  • Return { ok: true } on success. Catch errors: code 11000 becomes { ok: false, error: 'duplicate order number <value>' }, anything else { ok: false, error: err.message }.
  • main() seeds SKU-A (price 10, stock 5) and SKU-B (price 4, stock 2), awaits both models' init(), then: places ORD-1001 for 2 x SKU-A and 1 x SKU-B and prints the stock afterwards; retries the same order number and prints the mapped error; attempts ORD-1003 for 1 x SKU-A and 9 x SKU-B and prints the error and the stock again to prove the rollback; and finally reads ORD-1001 back with populate of lines.product selecting sku -_id, using lean(), printing the order number, total and one line per item.

Stock printing must use the helper the scaffold provides so the format is fixed.
exercises.inputSpec: No stdin. MONGO_URL must point at a replica set (a single-node replica set is fine) because transactions are used. Seed: SKU-A price 10 stock 5, SKU-B price 4 stock 2.
exercises.outputSpec: Lines: "stock: A=5 B=2"; "order1: ok"; "stock: A=3 B=1"; "duplicate: duplicate order number ORD-1001"; "oversell: insufficient stock for SKU-B"; "stock: A=3 B=1"; "read back: ORD-1001 total=24"; "line: SKU-A x2 @10"; "line: SKU-B x1 @4"; "lean has save: false".
exercises.constraints: The stock decrement must be conditional inside the update filter — never read-then-write, which is a lost-update race. Every read and write inside the transaction must carry the session. Do not roll back by hand; throwing inside withTransaction aborts. Keep model definitions out of solution.js.

examplesJson.input: await placeOrder({ orderNumber: 'ORD-1001', customer: 'ada', items: [{ sku: 'SKU-A', qty: 2 }, { sku: 'SKU-B', qty: 1 }] })
examplesJson.explanation: Both decrements and the order insert commit together; total is computed by the pre-validate hook as 2*10 + 1*4 = 24.

examplesJson.input: await placeOrder({ orderNumber: 'ORD-1001', customer: 'bob', items: [{ sku: 'SKU-A', qty: 1 }] })
examplesJson.explanation: The unique index on orderNumber raises 11000 inside the transaction, which aborts it, so the SKU-A decrement is rolled back too.

examplesJson.input: await placeOrder({ orderNumber: 'ORD-1003', customer: 'ada', items: [{ sku: 'SKU-A', qty: 1 }, { sku: 'SKU-B', qty: 9 }] })
examplesJson.output:
oversell: insufficient stock for SKU-B
stock: A=3 B=1
examplesJson.explanation: SKU-A was already decremented when SKU-B failed; because the throw aborts the transaction, the stock is unchanged afterwards — this is the whole point of the session.

hintsJson: Two writes that must both happen or neither need one unit of work around them.
session.withTransaction(fn) commits when fn resolves and aborts when it throws — you never call abortTransaction yourself.
Reads use .session(session); writes take { session } in their options; Order.create needs the array form to accept it.

const res = await Product.updateOne({ _id: product._id, stock: { $gte: item.qty } }, { $inc: { stock: -item.qty } }, { session }); if (res.modifiedCount !== 1) throw new Error('insufficient stock for ' + item.sku);

starterCodeJson.name: models.js
solution.js

solutionCodeJson.name: models.js
solution.js

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The transaction is the spine of the design. session.withTransaction(fn) starts a transaction, runs fn, commits if it resolves and aborts if it throws — and it retries the whole callback on transient errors such as a write conflict, which is why you must never do non-idempotent work outside the database inside that callback. Because the callback throws on the oversell, both the SKU-A decrement and any partial state vanish, and the stock printed afterwards is still A=3 B=1. Every operation must be told about the session (.session(session) for the query builder, { session } in write options), and this is the single most common bug in transactional Mongoose code: an operation that forgets the session runs outside the transaction, is not rolled back, and silently breaks atomicity. Model.create only accepts options when given an array, hence create([doc], { session }).

The stock decrement deserves its own attention. Reading product.stock, comparing it in Node and then writing the new value is a lost-update race: two concurrent orders both read 5 and both write 3. Putting the condition in the filter — { _id, stock: { $gte: qty } } with $inc — makes the check and the write one atomic server-side operation, so a failed condition simply matches nothing and modifiedCount is 0. That pattern is correct even without a transaction, and it is what lets the code detect insufficient stock without a separate read.

The error mapping shows the two failure classes staying distinct. The duplicate order number is enforced by the unique index (built via Order.init() before any write) and arrives as code 11000, mapped to a domain message; business-rule failures arrive as ordinary Errors carrying their message. An Express handler on top of this needs no knowledge of Mongo internals — it maps ok: false to a 409 or 422. Splitting models.js from the service is what makes that possible, and it also avoids OverwriteModelError by ensuring each model is compiled exactly once no matter how many routers import it.

Finally, the read-back combines populate on the nested path lines.product — populate follows dotted paths into subdocument arrays, still one extra query for the whole array — with lean(), since the response is serialised and never modified. Note the interaction the output makes explicit: a lean result has no save(), so a lean read is a deliberate one-way door. And remember the operational precondition: transactions need a replica set, so a standalone mongod will reject startTransaction outright, which is why local development mirrors production with a single-node replica set.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
sequenceDiagram
  participant Svc as placeOrder
  participant S as Session
  participant DB as MongoDB
  Svc->>S: startSession then withTransaction
  Svc->>DB: findOne product by sku with session
  Svc->>DB: updateOne with filter stock gte qty and inc
  DB-->>Svc: modifiedCount 1 means reserved
  Svc->>DB: create order with session
  Note over Svc,DB: any throw aborts and rolls back every write
  Svc->>S: commit then endSession
